one_line_takeaway: Off-the-shelf 2023-era LLMs hallucinate on 58-88% of verifiable questions about random U.S. federal cases, worsen at lower courts and on harder tasks, sycophantically accept false legal premises, and are overconfident when wrong -- so unsupervised use in legal practice is unsafe, especially for pro se litigants.
key_finding: Legal hallucination is pervasive even under the most conservative (temperature-0) decoding. Pooled across all reference-based tasks, mean hallucination rates run from 58% (GPT-4) to 88% (Llama 2), with GPT-3.5 at 69% and PaLM 2 at 72% (Figure 1). Rates rise monotonically with task complexity and as one descends the federal hierarchy (SCOTUS < USCOA < USDC), and fall for more prominent (high-PageRank) and mid-20th-century cases. On the high-complexity Doctrinal-agreement binary task, few-shot rates hover near 0.45-0.50 -- no better than random guessing -- indicating little grasp of substantive doctrine. Two further failure modes compound the risk: models frequently accept false legal premises (contra-factual/sycophantic behavior), and they are systematically OVERCONFIDENT, always erring in the positive direction. Notably, GPT-4 is best on raw hallucination yet only third of four on calibration (pooled ECE 0.190 vs PaLM 2's 0.057), meaning that when GPT-4 does hallucinate it is more likely to mislead. The paper cautions against rapid, unsupervised integration of LLMs into legal practice, with the greatest risk falling on those who would benefit most -- pro se litigants.
core_contribution: The first large-scale, systematic empirical profiling of FACTUAL legal hallucination in general-purpose, off-the-shelf LLMs. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a typology of legal hallucinations (Table 1) distinguishing three modes -- inconsistency with the prompt (closed-domain/intrinsic), inconsistency with the training corpus (open-domain/extrinsic), and inconsistency with the facts of the world (open-domain factual) -- of which the authors study ONLY the third; (2) a 14-task legal QA suite (Table 2) organized into low/moderate/high complexity tiers (e.g., case Existence, Court, Citation, Author, Disposition, Quotation, Authority, Overruling year, Doctrinal agreement, Factual background, Central holding); (3) a purpose-built evaluation dataset of 15,000 U.S. federal cases (5,000 each from SCOTUS, USCOA, USDC) drawn by stratified random sampling and merged with external metadata to form a test oracle, released on HuggingFace and Harvard Dataverse; (4) two estimators -- a reference-based hallucination rate (Eq. 1) using ground-truth metadata, and a reference-free contradiction-based lower-bound estimator (Eq. 2) for tasks lacking tabular ground truth; and (5) empirical findings that hallucinations rise with task complexity and with descent down the judicial hierarchy, plus two additional failure modes: contra-factual bias (sycophantic acceptance of false legal premises) and systematic overconfidence/miscalibration.
threat_model: None. This is a measurement/reliability paper with no adversarial threat model, no attacker, and no exploit or defense. The harms it documents -- pro se and under-resourced litigants being misled by confident false statements of law, a 'legal monoculture' that flattens jurisdictional and temporal nuance, and inductive biases propagating into downstream tools built on these foundation models -- arise from unsupervised reliance on unreliable models, not from a malicious actor. The contra-factual (sycophancy) finding is framed as an honest-user error mode (a researcher unknowingly embedding a false premise), NOT as an injection attack surface. Per the task's guardrail against forcing a security frame, no threat model is invented here.
limitations: (1) Reference-free hallucination rates for the five high-complexity tasks are LOWER BOUNDS only (self-contradiction guarantees hallucination, but consistency does not guarantee truth), and those cells use only n=100 cases per court level. (2) The Existence task is inflated by the models' tendency to always answer 'yes' (they used only real cases; fake-case robustness is relegated to the Online Appendix). (3) The SCOTUS Subsequent-history rate is understated because the contradiction method cannot catch repeated-but-wrong answers (e.g., models uniformly saying litigation ended at the Supreme Court). (4) Llama 2's near-zero contra-factual hallucination (0.02) is an ARTIFACT of premise rejection -- it often denies the real case/justice exists -- not genuine robustness, and this same tendency drives its worst-in-class raw performance. (5) Models are the 2023-era snapshots (gpt-4-1106-preview, gpt-3.5-turbo-0613, text-bison-001, Llama-2-13b-chat-hf); no retrieval augmentation is tested (RAG and other mitigations are discussed only as CITED third-party literature, with caveats, not proposed or evaluated by the authors). (6) Reliance on GPT-4 as the contradiction judge introduces a model-in-the-loop dependency (mitigated but not eliminated by the human validation).

datasets_used: Author-constructed legal-hallucination benchmark: 15,000 U.S. federal cases (5,000 SCOTUS 1794-2015, 5,000 USCOA 1895-2019, 5,000 USDC 1932-2019) via stratified random sampling (year / circuit-year / state-year strata); released on HuggingFace (reglab/legal_hallucinations) and Harvard Dataverse
Overruling-year subset: 279 explicitly overruled SCOTUS cases (1810-2022)
Doctrinal-agreement set: 5,000 SCOTUS citing-cited case pairs (2,839 coded 'agree', 2,161 'disagree'), 1796-2005, from Shepard's treatment codes
High-complexity reference-free tasks evaluated on n=100 cases per court level

Metadata sources merged to build the oracle: Caselaw Access Project (2023), Supreme Court Database (Spaeth et al.), Appeals Courts Database Project, Library of Congress / Congress.gov overruled-decisions table, Shepard's Citations
quantitative_results: Pooled reference-based mean hallucination rate (Fig 1): GPT-4 0.58, GPT-3.5 0.69, PaLM 2 0.72, Llama 2 0.88
High-complexity reference-free LOWER BOUNDS: Factual background & Procedural posture >=49%; Core legal question >=59%; Central holding >=63%
Doctrinal agreement (binary, few-shot): ~0.45-0.50 across models -- no better than random (0.5)
External comparison point: Mundler et al. found GPT-3.5 self-contradicted ~14.3% on general QA; legal rates far exceed this
Contra-factual bias pooled (Fig 8): GPT-4 0.69, GPT-3.5 0.52, PaLM 2 0.99, Llama 2 0.02 (0.02 is a premise-rejection artifact, not robustness)
Pooled Expected Calibration Error: PaLM 2 0.057, GPT-3.5 0.099, GPT-4 0.190, Llama 2 0.421; all error is in the positive (overconfident) direction
Low-complexity few-shot Court task hallucination: 0.000 SCOTUS -> ~0.49-0.70 USCOA -> ~0.83-0.87 USDC (rising down the hierarchy)
baselines_compared: Four LLMs compared against one another: GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview), GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-0613), PaLM 2 (text-bison-001), Llama 2 (Llama-2-13b-chat-hf)
Random-guessing floor of 0.5 for binary tasks (Disposition, Doctrinal agreement)
External reference: Mundler et al. 2023 general-QA self-contradiction rate ~14.3% (a comparison point, not a baseline method the authors built)
Zero-shot vs three-shot prompting compared within each model (found not to differ substantially)
